1: wayyāḇōʾ hammeleḵ wəHāmān lišṯôṯ ʾim-ʾEsṯēr hammalkāh — 'The king and Haman came to drink with Esther the queen.' The verb šāṯāh (H8354 = to drink) governs the social frame: this is the 'drinking banquet' (mišṯeh, from the same root) that Esther had prepared. The pairing of the king and Haman as joint guests at the queen's table is the formal setting for the denouement. The repetition of the banquet setting (cf. Esther 5:4-8) doubles the dramatic tension: Esther's first banquet produced a second invitation; the second banquet will produce the revelation she has been preparing.
2: wayyōʾmer hammeleḵ ləʾEsṯēr gam bayyôm haššēnî bəmišṯēh hayyāyin — 'And the king said to Esther again on the second day at the wine banquet.' māh šəʾēlāṯēḵ ʾEsṯēr hammalkāh wəṯinnāṯēn lāḵ ûmāh baqqāšāṯēḵ ʿaḏ-ḥăṣî hammalkûṯ wəṯēʿāś — 'What is your request, Queen Esther? It shall be granted you. What is your petition? Even to half the kingdom, it shall be done.' The Persian royal formula ʿaḏ-ḥăṣî hammalkûṯ ('up to half the kingdom') appears three times in Esther (Esther 5:3, Esther 5:6, Esther 7:2) — a rhetorical hyperbole of unlimited royal generosity. The irony is that what Esther will request is not territory or wealth but lives, which no royal formula can technically grant without overturning a standing decree.
3: wattagʿan ʾEsṯēr hammalkāh watōʾmar ʾim-māṣāʾṯî ḥēn bəʿênêḵā hammeleḵ — 'And Queen Esther answered and said: If I have found favor in your eyes, O king.' The conditional clause ʾim-māṣāʾṯî ḥēn (if I have found favor) is Esther's standard rhetorical opener — deferential, petition-form language (cf. Esther 5:8; Esther 8:5). wəʾim-ʿal-hammeleḵ ṭôḇ — 'and if it pleases the king' — the second condition stacks the deference. tinnāṯen-lî napšî bišʾēlāṯî wəʿammî bəḇaqqāšāṯî — 'let my life (napšî) be given to me at my petition and my people (ʿammî) at my request.' Nepsheš (H5315 = soul, life, living being) is the first-person self-presentation: Esther has identified herself with her people's fate. The paired structure — my life / my people — makes her petition both personal and communal.
4: kî nimkarnû ʾănî wəʿammî lhašmîḏ lahărôg ûləʾabbēḏ — 'For we have been sold — I and my people — to be destroyed, to be killed, and to be annihilated.' Māḵar (H4376, niphal = to be sold) picks up the commercial language of Haman's decree-purchase (Esther 3:9 — 'I will pay 10,000 talents'). Lhašmîḏ lahărôg ûləʾabbēḏ — the three-term genocidal formula (H8045 + H2026 + H6) repeats the decree's language from 3:13 exactly, making the connection unmistakable. wəʾilû laʿăḇāḏîm ûlišpāḥôṯ nimkarnû heḥěrašṯî — 'If we had merely been sold as slaves, male and female, I would have been silent.' Esther's rhetorical move: she would not trouble the king for the loss of freedom (economic harm to the king), but extinction cannot be borne in silence. Heḥěrašṯî (H2790, hiphil = to be silent, hold the tongue) — the contrast between silent acceptance of slavery and the impossibility of silence before genocide.
5: wayyōʾmer hammeleḵ ʾAḥašwērôš wayyōʾmer ləʾEsṯēr hammalkāh mî hûʾ zeh wəʾê-zeh hûʾ ʾăšer-məlāʾô libbô laʿăśôṯ kēn — 'King Ahasuerus said, and he said to Queen Esther: Who is he, and where is he who has dared to do this?' The doubled verb wayyōʾmer (he said... and he said) is unusual — possibly indicating that the king was stunned into a pause, or reflecting the intensity of his reaction. The phrase ʾăšer-məlāʾô libbô — 'whose heart has filled him (to do this)' = 'who dared' — uses the heart-fullness idiom for audacity or presumption. The king's outrage is oriented toward the actor, not yet identified: 'who did this?'
6: watōʾmer ʾEsṯēr ʾîš ṣar wəʾôyēḇ Hāmān hārāʿ hazzeh — 'Esther said: A man of distress and an enemy — this wicked Haman!' The paired accusation formula ṣar wəʾôyēḇ (H6887 = adversary/one who presses + H341 = enemy) is the full legal indictment: not merely a rival but an active enemy. Hāmān hārāʿ hazzeh — 'this wicked Haman' — the demonstrative hazzeh (this) used deictically to point at the person present. The naming comes after the characterization: first the moral category, then the personal identification. wəHāmān nib'at millipnê hammeleḵ wəhammalkāh — 'and Haman was terrified before the king and the queen.' Bāʿat (H1204, niphal = to be terrified, startled) — the instantaneous fear-response upon being named.
7: wəhammeleḵ qām baḥămāṯô mimmišṯēh hayyāyin ʾel-gannath habbîṯān — 'And the king rose in his wrath from the wine banquet to the palace garden.' Ḥēmāh (H2534 = wrath, burning anger) — the same word used of the king's anger at Vashti (Esther 1:12) and at the first report of Haman's plot against Mordecai (after Esther's revelation). Gannath habbîṯān — 'the garden of the palace' (H1594 + H1055 = the enclosed garden attached to the palace complex). The king's withdrawal to the garden is a moment of processing: he has condemned Haman but not yet decided the execution. wəHāmān ʿāmaḏ ləḇaqqēš ʿal-napšô mēʾEsṯēr hammalkāh — 'and Haman stood to beg for his life from Esther the queen.' Baqqēš ʿal-nepheš (to seek for one's life) — the ultimate reversal: the man who sought to destroy the queen's people now begs the queen for his own life.
8: wəhammeleḵ šāḇ migan habbîṯān ʾel-bêṯ mišṯēh hayyāyin — 'And the king returned from the palace garden to the house of the wine banquet.' The return from garden to banquet-room signals that the king has reached his decision. wəHāmān nōpēl ʿal-hammittāh ʾăšer ʾEsṯēr ʿālêhā — 'and Haman had fallen upon the couch where Esther was.' Nāpal (H5307 = to fall, throw oneself) in the supplication position: prostrating at the queen's reclining couch in the Persian banquet setting. The king's interpretation: hăgam liḵbôš ʾeṯ-hammalkāh ʿimmî babbāyiṯ — 'Is he also going to violate the queen in my house?' Kāḇaš (H3533 = to subdue, force, violate) — the king's reading of Haman's prostration as sexual assault. Whether intentional misreading or genuine misunderstanding, the king's declaration seals Haman's fate. hadāḇār yaṣāʾ mippî hammeleḵ ûpənê Hāmān ḥāp̄û — 'the word went out of the king's mouth and they covered Haman's face.' Ḥāp̄û (H2645, piel = to cover) — the covering of the condemned man's face was an ancient execution protocol; Haman is effectively dead at this moment.
9: wayyōʾmer Harbônāh ʾaḥaḏ min-hassārîsîm lipnê hammeleḵ — 'Then Harbona, one of the eunuchs in the king's presence, said.' Harbona is one of the seven eunuchs listed in Esther 1:10. His intervention provides the information that triggers the execution method. gam hinnēh-hāʿēṣ ʾăšer-ʿāśāh Hāmān ləMordoḵay ʾăšer dibbēr-ṭôḇ ʿal-hammeleḵ ʿōmēḏ bêṯ Hāmān gāḇōah ḥămiššîm ʾammāh — 'Also, look — the tree/gallows that Haman made for Mordecai, who spoke good for the king, stands in Haman's house, 50 cubits high.' Hāʿēṣ (H6086 = tree, wood, wooden stake/gallows) — the very instrument Haman constructed for Mordecai becomes his own execution apparatus. The fifty-cubit height (approximately 75 feet) signals that it was visible above the city's rooflines — designed for maximum public spectacle. The subordinate clause 'who spoke good for the king' is Harbona's reminder of Mordecai's unrewarded loyalty. wayyōʾmer hammeleḵ tālethu ʿālāyw — 'And the king said: Hang him on it.'
10: wayyillu ʾeṯ-Hāmān ʿal-hāʿēṣ ʾăšer-hēḵîn ləMordoḵay — 'And they hanged Haman on the gallows that he had prepared for Mordecai.' The sentence is structured so that the relative clause 'which he had prepared for Mordecai' immediately follows the description of the execution — the instrument of intended murder becomes the instrument of actual execution. Kûn (H3559, hiphil = to prepare, establish, make ready) in the perfect tense emphasizes that it was Haman's own preparation, completed and ready. waḥămaṯ hammeleḵ šāḵāḵāh — 'And the king's wrath subsided.' Šāḵak (H7918 = to become quiet, subside, be appeased) — the same verb as the ocean subsiding after Jonah's casting overboard (Jonah 1:11,12). The king's wrath is satisfied by the execution that the king's own servant provided the means for, using the perpetrator's own construction.
